purpose: Global content taxonomy for Sofia. This file maps service topics and investigation issues to section-level content intelligence so Sofia can produce problem-first, investigation-focused content instead of generic polygraph explanations.

corporate_investigations.description: Company, employee, loss, fraud, misconduct, and internal investigation topics.

human_resources.description: Recruitment, integrity screening, periodic employee testing, and workplace trust topics.

private_cases.description: Relationship, family, and personal trust topics.

sexual_offense_related.description: Sensitive sexual allegation, defense, monitoring, and treatment-support topics.

legal_applications.description: Lawyer, defense, case preparation, credibility, criminal, and civil legal-support topics.

problem: missing cash, stock, tools, equipment, documents, or company property
management may know that a loss occurred but not who had direct involvement
suspicion may fall on several employees with access or opportunity
the company may need to act without damaging trust or accusing the wrong person
consequences: repeated losses if responsibility is not clarified
investigation_challenges: documents may show a loss but not the person responsible
several employees may have access to the same area, keys, cash, system, or stock
employees may deny involvement or provide conflicting explanations
witnesses may be reluctant to speak
the company must distinguish error, negligence, poor controls, and deliberate theft
polygraph_role: may help clarify specific questions about participation, knowledge, concealment, or benefit
can support a broader internal investigation when combined with documents, access records, interviews, and stock controls
should focus on clearly defined incidents rather than general suspicion
should not be presented as proof of guilt
limitations: must be voluntary
does not replace a full internal investigation
should not be the only basis for disciplinary action
results must be interpreted within the full case context
faq: Can a polygraph replace an internal theft investigation?
What if several employees had access to the missing property?
Can the examination clarify whether an employee participated in a specific theft?
Should a company discipline an employee based only on a polygraph result?
What information should be collected before requesting the examination?

problem: unexplained fuel consumption in transport or logistics operations
differences between mileage, route plans, fuel card use, and actual fuel purchases
fuel records showing a loss without clearly showing whether the cause is waste, poor controls, error, negligence, or deliberate diversion
management uncertainty about whether the issue involves drivers, supervisors, suppliers, fuel card users, or internal logistics staff
the need to investigate without making premature accusations against employees
consequences: higher operating costs and reduced fleet profitability
loss of confidence in fuel records, route data, and internal controls
workplace tension when suspicion falls on several employees
risk of unfair accusations if the company acts before clarifying the facts
difficulty making disciplinary, operational, or audit decisions while responsibility remains unclear
investigation_challenges: the loss may be visible in fuel records, but the records may not show who caused it
GPS data, fuel cards, invoices, route sheets, and mileage logs may show discrepancies without proving intent
several people may have access to vehicles, cards, receipts, tanks, or supplier contacts
the company may need to distinguish poor control, operational waste, negligence, collusion, and deliberate diversion
employees may provide conflicting explanations for the same fuel discrepancy
technical monitoring may identify where the loss appears but not whether a person knowingly participated
polygraph_role: may help clarify specific statements about participation, knowledge, assistance, concealment, or benefit from fuel diversion
should focus on defined issues such as unauthorised sale, fictitious fuel purchase, misuse of fuel cards, or concealment of known irregularities
can support a broader internal investigation when combined with fleet records, fuel audits, GPS data, invoices, access logs, and interviews
should not be presented as proof of fraud or as a replacement for operational and financial controls
limitations: should be used only with voluntary examinees
should focus on specific, reviewable issues rather than general suspicion
does not replace accounting, GPS, fleet, supplier, or fuel-control evidence
results must be interpreted within the full context of the investigation
faq: Can a polygraph replace a fuel audit?
What if several drivers used the same vehicle or fuel card?
Can the examination clarify whether a driver knowingly participated in fuel diversion?
Can the examination be used when records show a discrepancy but not who caused it?
Should the company rely only on a polygraph result before taking disciplinary action?
What information should the company collect before requesting an examination?

problem: stock discrepancies between warehouse records and physical inventory
possible manipulation of entries, transfers, returns, damaged goods, write-offs, or stock adjustments
uncertainty about whether the problem is error, weak control, negligence, theft, or deliberate manipulation
several departments may be involved, including warehouse, purchasing, logistics, accounting, and management
consequences: hidden theft or fraud continuing over time
loss of confidence in inventory systems
investigation_challenges: many employees may interact with stock before a discrepancy is discovered
records may show an inconsistency without showing intent
errors and deliberate manipulation may appear similar
responsibility may be divided between receiving, storage, dispatch, returns, and record-keeping
employees may blame system errors or procedural confusion
polygraph_role: may clarify whether specific individuals knowingly manipulated records, concealed losses, or participated in unauthorised stock movement
should focus on defined acts, dates, transactions, or events
can support internal interviews, audits, and stock-control reviews
should not replace a proper inventory audit
limitations: requires precise question formulation
must be part of a broader investigation
should not be used to test vague suspicion
must respect voluntary participation and professional limits
faq: Can a polygraph replace an inventory audit?
What if several employees handled the same stock?
Can the examination clarify whether records were knowingly manipulated?
What documents should be reviewed before the examination?
Can the test distinguish error from deliberate manipulation?

problem: suspicions involving inflated invoices, false suppliers, kickbacks, favoritism, or manipulated purchasing decisions
uncertainty about whether a decision was poor management, weak control, negligence, conflict of interest, or deliberate misconduct
concerns involving employees, suppliers, intermediaries, or third-party contractors
consequences: damage to governance and internal controls
investigation_challenges: irregularities may be hidden behind apparently valid paperwork
several people may request, approve, process, or receive goods or services
supplier relationships can make responsibility difficult to determine
employees may claim decisions were normal business judgment
financial records may show a transaction but not the intent behind it
polygraph_role: may clarify specific questions about knowledge, benefit, concealment, or involvement in defined transactions
can support compliance, audit, or internal investigation processes
should focus on specific suppliers, invoices, approvals, or payments
should not replace legal review, financial audit, or compliance investigation
limitations: does not prove corruption or legal liability
must avoid vague or overly broad questions
requires voluntary participation
results must be evaluated with documentary evidence
faq: Can a polygraph replace a procurement audit?
Can the examination address suspected kickbacks or supplier favoritism?
What if several employees approved the same transaction?
Can the examination clarify whether an employee knowingly concealed a conflict of interest?
Should lawyers or compliance officers be involved?

problem: allegations of misconduct inside the workplace
conflicting accounts between employees
management pressure to respond fairly without relying only on rumor
sensitive facts that may affect discipline, trust, or employee relations
consequences: risk of unfair action if facts are not clarified
investigation_challenges: witnesses may be reluctant
accounts may conflict
documentation may be limited
sensitive allegations require careful handling
disciplinary action requires fairness and proportionality
polygraph_role: may assist in clarifying specific factual issues when appropriate
can support a broader HR or investigative process
should be used carefully with consent and professional boundaries
should not replace HR or legal advice
limitations: not suitable for every HR situation
must respect dignity, voluntariness, and confidentiality
should not be used as pressure
must be limited to relevant factual issues
faq: Can a polygraph be used in a workplace misconduct investigation?

Is participation voluntary?
Can the result be the only basis for disciplinary action?
When should HR or legal counsel be involved?

problem: suspected disclosure of confidential information to unauthorised parties
concerns involving client lists, pricing, contracts, strategy, internal documents, or trade secrets
difficulty determining whether information was leaked intentionally, accidentally, or through weak controls
consequences: internal suspicion among employees with access
investigation_challenges: digital access logs may show who accessed information but not intent
several employees may have legitimate access
information may be copied, photographed, forwarded, or discussed informally
employees may deny disclosure or claim accidental handling
polygraph_role: may clarify specific questions about unauthorised disclosure, knowledge, or concealment
can support digital forensic review, access-log analysis, and internal interviews
should focus on clearly defined information and timeframes
limitations: does not replace digital forensics or legal review
must avoid vague questions about loyalty or character
should focus on specific acts and voluntary participation
faq: Can a polygraph help investigate a confidential information leak?
What if several employees had access to the same documents?
Can the test clarify whether information was shared intentionally?
Should digital evidence be reviewed first?

concerns involving employees, supervisors, or approvers

limitations: does not replace expense audit

polygraph_role: may clarify specific questions about undisclosed benefit, knowledge, or concealment
can support compliance review and internal interviews
should focus on defined relationships, transactions, or benefits

problem: employers may need to assess integrity risk before hiring for sensitive positions
concerns may involve theft history, drug use, falsified qualifications, confidential information, conflicts of interest, or undisclosed misconduct
the challenge is to make hiring decisions responsibly without relying only on assumptions
consequences: hiring people into sensitive roles without adequate integrity assessment may create financial, security, or reputational risk
poor screening can expose companies to theft, fraud, data leaks, or workplace misconduct
unfair or poorly designed screening can harm candidates and expose the company to ethical or legal problems
investigation_challenges: CVs, references, and interviews may not reveal all relevant integrity concerns
some information may be difficult to verify independently
screening must be proportionate, voluntary, and related to the role
questions must avoid irrelevant personal areas
polygraph_role: may support integrity screening when role relevance, consent, and proper question formulation exist
should focus on job-relevant topics such as prior theft, falsification, unauthorised disclosure, or serious policy violations
must be part of a broader recruitment and background-check process
limitations: should not replace interviews, reference checks, or background checks
must be legally and ethically appropriate for the country and role
requires voluntary participation and clear explanation
faq: Can a polygraph be used before hiring?
What kinds of questions are appropriate in pre-employment screening?
Can the test replace background checks?
Is candidate consent required?
Should all roles be screened or only sensitive positions?

problem: companies may need periodic integrity monitoring for employees in sensitive roles
concerns may involve access to cash, inventory, confidential data, security, clients, fuel, or high-value assets
the goal is to manage risk without creating unnecessary pressure or distrust
consequences: undetected misconduct may continue over time
poorly managed testing can damage morale
inconsistent screening may create perceptions of unfairness
risk management must be balanced with ethics and employee dignity
investigation_challenges: maintenance testing must be policy-based, not arbitrary
questions must be role-relevant and specific
participation, consent, and communication must be handled carefully
testing should not replace supervision, controls, audits, or HR processes
polygraph_role: may support integrity monitoring in high-trust or sensitive positions
can help assess specific policy-related issues when properly designed
should be integrated into a broader compliance, HR, or security framework
limitations: not suitable for every workplace
must be transparent, voluntary, and professionally managed
should not be used as intimidation or routine pressure
faq: What is employee maintenance testing?
Which roles may justify periodic integrity testing?
Can maintenance testing replace internal controls?
How should employees be informed?
What topics are appropriate for maintenance examinations?

problem: senior employees may have access to money, strategy, confidential data, clients, suppliers, and decision-making authority
companies may need additional confidence before placing someone in a high-trust role
concerns may include conflicts of interest, financial misconduct, data disclosure, or undisclosed integrity risks
consequences: executive misconduct can cause strategic, financial, reputational, and governance damage
poor screening at senior level may affect the entire organisation
loss of trust in leadership can be difficult to repair
investigation_challenges: senior candidates may have complex histories and wide business networks
some integrity concerns may not appear in ordinary references
screening must be proportionate and professionally justified
questions must remain job-relevant
polygraph_role: may support broader executive due diligence when legally and ethically appropriate
should focus on defined job-relevant integrity issues
must be integrated with background checks, interviews, and references
limitations: does not replace due diligence
must respect consent and legal boundaries
should not be used for irrelevant personal intrusion
faq: Can a polygraph be used for executive screening?
What topics are appropriate for senior-role screening?
Can the examination replace background checks?
Is executive screening suitable for every company?

problem: organisations may need to assess trustworthiness in roles involving assets, data, security, money, or vulnerable clients
integrity concerns may appear before hiring, after incidents, or during periodic risk reviews
the challenge is to assess risk responsibly without overreaching
consequences: weak integrity controls may expose the organisation to theft, fraud, abuse, leaks, or misconduct
excessive or poorly designed testing may damage trust and fairness
integrity programs require clear boundaries and professional standards
investigation_challenges: integrity is broad and must be translated into specific, relevant questions
general character questions are usually less useful than defined conduct questions
testing must be proportionate to role risk and case context
polygraph_role: may support integrity assessment when focused on specific job-relevant conduct
can complement interviews, background checks, audits, and internal controls
should not be used as a broad character judgment
limitations: must avoid vague moral judgments
requires clear consent and relevance
should be interpreted as part of a broader assessment

Which topics are appropriate for integrity testing?
Can integrity testing be used before hiring?
Can it be used for existing employees?

accusations can escalate emotional conflict

can support a structured conversation about defined issues
should focus on clear conduct questions rather than broad emotional judgments

limitations: should not be used to pressure a partner
does not solve relationship problems by itself
requires consent and emotional care
questions must avoid humiliation or unnecessary personal intrusion
